docs.text: Hello! I'm an Assistant Professor in the Department of Aerospace Engineering at Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay. I'm interested in understanding, modeling and simulating the mechanics and thermodynamics of solids, with a special focus on the physics of various kinds of defects in crystalline solids. You will find more information about my research and teaching activities at IIT Bombay, and about the people in my research group, in the following pages. Here is a brief summary of my academic background: 2001-2006: B.Tech/M.Tech Dual Degree (Aerospace Engineering) - IIT Madras . 2006-2012: Ph.D. (Aerospace Engineering) - Indian Institute of Science . 2012 (May - July): Visiting Researcher - University of Exeter . 2012-2013: Post-doctoral researcher - Ecole Centrale de Nantes . 2014-2017: Post-doctoral researcher - California Institute of Technology . 2018-Present: Assistant Professor (Aerospace Engineering) - IIT Bombay . Outside academic research, I enjoy listening to Carnatic music (the classical music of south India), and occassionally play it on the fretless electric guitar.
docs.title: Home

docs.text: Here are additional details of my current and past research activities. A new iterative scheme to solve the Peierls-Nabarro model The Peierls-Nabarro model is one of the earliest, and still widely used, examples of a multiscale model that aims at predicting the core structure of dislocations in crystalline materials. The key idea behind the Peierls-Nabarro model is that the energy of a dislocated crystal can be thought of as the sum of the bulk elastic energy and the surface energy associated with the slip plane bounded by the dislocation. Despite its simplicity and shortcomings, the Peierls-Nabarro model is one of the most successful continuum model of dislocation cores. Dissociation of edge and screw dislocations on {111} planes in Aluminium. In a recent work , I proposed a new iterative scheme to solve a generalized version, due to Schoeck, of the Peierls-Nabarro model. Unlike existing techniques, the proposed method exploits certain properties of the Hilbert transform to reduces the solution of the Peierls-Nabarro model to a simple fixed point iteration scheme. An application of the new iterative scheme to study the dissociated core structure of both edge and screw dislocations on {111} planes in Aluminium is illustrated above. Check out the preprint for more details. Nano-void growth mechanism in Magnesium As part of my post-doctoral research at Caltech, funded by the MEDE program, I studied spallation in Magnesium (Mg) using the quasicontinuum method . The interest in Mg is primarily due to its promise as a lightweight alternative to conventional metals for various engineering applications. Mg naturally occurs in the hexagonal close packed (HCP) crystal structure. The HCP structure has low symmetry in comparison to other crystal structures like the face centered cubic (FCC) structure, and this has specific implications on how plastic deformation is accommodated at the atomic scale. Unlike FCC materials where slip systems dominate, the role of twinning is crucial for understanding the plastic response of HCP materials. Plasticity around nano-voids in Magnesium To understand the atomistic basis of spall, we (I, along with Mauricio Ponga , Kaushik Bhattacharya and Michael Ortiz ) studied the time evolution of nano-voids in single crystal Mg subject to tensile strain loads. This study led to the important conclusion that the absence of any significant interactions between dislocation activity on the basal plane, involving basal slip and prismatic loop formation, and $\langle c+a \rangle$ dislocations that accommodate deformation perpendicular to the basal plane, plays a crucial role in the observed lower spall strength of Mg. Check out our paper for more details

docs.title: Contact

docs.text: Research Interests I'm primarily interested in multiscale modeling of the mechanical response of materials. A proper understanding of the mechanics and thermodynamics of materials requires a careful analysis of phenomena occurring across a wide span of length and time scales, from the sub-atomic to the continuum, and their scale spanning interactions. A faithful modeling of the material behavior thus necessitates the use of very different models at different scales: quantum mechanics at the sub-atomic scale, classical mechanics at the atomic scale, statistical mechanics at the mesoscale, and effective continuum models at the macro scale. I draw upon tools from mathematics, physics, computation and data science, to explore some of these multiscale aspects of the mechanics and thermodynamics of materials. I'm also interested in certain aspects of wave propagation in solids . More details about my current and previous research activities can be found on this page, and on my research blog . docs.text: Openings PhD students I'm looking to hire one or more PhD students in the broad areas of multiscale modeling of constitutive models for crystalline materials, application of machine learning in materials science, high performance scientific computing in the context of atomistic simulations, and certain theoretical aspects of inelastic behavior. Please write to me in case you are interested. In additional to students with degrees in aerospace/mechanical/civil engineering, I strongly encourage students with degrees in physics or mathematics to also apply. I'm especially interested in students with a strong mathematical background since most of my research involves a heavy dose of mathematics in one form or the other. JRF There is an opening in the group for a project on the Quasicontinuum Method which is funded by the Department of Science and Technology. I'm looking for candidates with a Batchelors or Masters degree who have a working knowledge of high performance scientific computing (MPI with C++), undergraduate level calculus and linear algebra. A background in atomistic simulation is preferable, but not necessary. This will be a full-time Research Assistant position at IIT Bombay. The candidates will be hired initially for a short term, and will be extended for upto two years if the performance is satisfactory. Send me an email with your CV in case you are interested and/or need more details. Summer and winter internships I'm not offering any internships at the moment. Please do not write to me about internship related queries. I will advertise openings for internships here if I can offer such positions in the future. Current group members Here is a list of students who are working with me on various projects. More details coming soon! PhD candidates Divyesh Mistry (Dislocation Dynamics) Undergraduate students Prasanth YSS (Geometric Mechanics and Variational Integrators) Anurag Patel (Molecular Dynamics with PySPH; joint student with Prof. Prabhu Ramachandran) Aaron John Sabu (Mechanical Logic Gates) Short-term student rojects Supervised Learning Project (SLP) Karthik Anantharaman (Topology Optimization) Prasanth YSS (Optimal Control) Apoorva Singh Gangwar (Random Walks) Rushi Lotti (Neural Networks) M.Tech. Seminars Vinay Padmajan (Metamaterials) Former members Ninad Bhat ( Now: Masters student at Australian National University) Dharamveer Kumar ( Now: Ph.D. candidate at University of Minnesota) Sanjoy Kumar Mazumder ( Now: Ph.D. candidate at Purdue University)

docs.text: Openings PhD students I'm looking to hire one or more PhD students in the broad areas of multiscale modeling of constitutive models for crystalline materials, application of machine learning in materials science, high performance scientific computing in the context of atomistic simulations, and certain theoretical aspects of inelastic behavior. Please see the Information for Prospective Students section below before you apply. In additional to students with degrees in aerospace/mechanical/civil engineering, I strongly encourage students with degrees in physics or mathematics to also apply. I'm especially interested in students with a strong mathematical background since most of my research involves a heavy dose of mathematics in one form or the other. JRF There is an opening in the group for a project on the Quasicontinuum Method which is funded by the Department of Science and Technology. I'm looking for candidates with a Batchelors or Masters degree who have a working knowledge of high performance scientific computing (MPI with C++), undergraduate level calculus and linear algebra. A background in atomistic simulation is preferable, but not necessary. This will be a full-time Research Assistant position at IIT Bombay. The candidates will be hired initially for a short term, and will be extended for upto two years if the performance is satisfactory. Send me an email with your CV in case you are interested and/or need more details. Summer and winter internships I'm not offering any internships at the moment. Please do not write to me about internship related queries. I will advertise openings for internships here if I can offer such positions in the future. Information for prospective students Note: The information below is intended for students who wish to work in my group only . I'm putting it out here to help you, the prospective student, to make an informed decision to either join or not join my group. The research in my group is broadly about understanding the physics of material behavior across various scales. Depending on the nature of the project, it can either be mathematics-intensive, or computation-intensive, or both. But before you commence your research in my group formally you need to be aware of the following guidelines: Scientific spirit: I expect you to fully assimilate the scientific spirit, understand the way scientific research is conducted and strive to embody the scientific spirit in your research. If you don't care strongly about the scientific spirit, then I don't think you should be doing a Ph.D. in the first place. You can find a very nice and simple introduction to the nature of the science in this link . Research ethics and integrity: It is imperative that you hold yourself to the highest ethical standards when it comes to research. Please read the guidelines given in this link to understand certain basic aspects of research ethics. Scientific integrity is an indispensable to doing good research; please be advised that there will be severe consequences in line with the institute policies if I find any evidence that you engaged in any form of academic misconduct. Growth mindset: Pursuing a Ph.D. is a serious time commitment from both your and my end. It is very probable that you may feel overwhelmed, overworked, or lost during the process. That is natural, and it is totally okay to feel like that. What is really important is that you learn how to cope with failures of various kinds, which are inevitable in your scientific journey. Rather than being dejected by failure, it really helps to embrace them as opportunities to re-evaluate the situation and improve yourself. If you join my group, you are always welcome to talk to me about any difficulty you may be facing. It is important for me that you feel welcome and find the atmosphere in the research group nurturing so that you may pursue your research to the best of your abilities. But I can't help you if you don't talk to me. While on this topic, I highly recommend reading Mindset: the new psychology of success by Carol Dweck. Work hard: Doing a Ph.D. in my group will definitely involve a lot of hard work. I can assure you that it will be quite challenging, but it will be well worth the effort in the end. My goal is to help you become a good and independent researcher by the time you graduate. If you don't like to work hard, I don't think you should join my group. I will, of course, make sure that you have the necessary resources and help to ensure that you enjoy the process, but there are no shortcuts in research: you need to work hard and you need to learn how to persist in the face of failures. In addition to the general guidelines mentioned above, you are also expected to take care of the following prerequisites on your own. You can, of course, talk to me if you need help with any of these. You are expected to learn the following by the end of the first semester of your Ph.D.: Scientific writing: The ability to write well and to express your ideas clearly is an integral part of doing good science. These skills do not sprout magically. They are rather acquired through deliberate effort and practice. You may find this resource to be a useful starting point. I will give you specific writing and presentation tasks over the course of your Ph.D. to help you develop these skills and learn the art of effective communication. LaTeX: The de-facto standard in preparing technical documents in science and engineering is the LaTeX typesetting system. You will be expected to turn in numerous reports over the course of your Ph.D., and, of course, communicate your reserach to other researchers in the form of peer-reviewed publications. Learning LaTeX will turn out to be very useful in this process. I expect you to learn how to create documents using LaTeX; you may consult this tutorial as a simple starting point. You can also use LaTeX to create presentations; see this tutorial to get started with using the Beamer package. Programming: I expect you have a hands-on knowledge of programming in Python irrespective of whether your project is theoretical or computational. A wonderful resource for learning Python is the video lecture series by Prof. Prabhu Ramachandran , who is a faculty member in the Aerospace Engineering department here at IIT Bombay. You are expected to know the material covered in the videos in the Basic-Programming-using-Python folder in the link give earlier. If your Ph.D. research is computational in nature, I expect you to know the basics of programming in C++, in addition to Python. A good tutorial for learning C++ can be found here . Plotting and visualization: Creating good plots and presenting data using effective visualization tools are an integral part of scientific communication. For quick and dirty plotting, I recommend learning Gnuplot; a tutorial can be found here . For creating publication quality plots, I recommend using PyPlot; you can find a good tutorial here . Scientific illustrations: Drawing good schematics provides a very elegant means to communicate various ideas and concepts in your scientific research. It is therefore important to learn how to create good illustrations. I recommend learning Ipe , which is a free and easy-to-use software for creating scientific illustrations. If you are more adventurous, you may optionally try learning the Tikz package; a basic set of tutorials can be found here . You will be doing a variety of coursework during the first year of your Ph.D. I will recommend various courses based on the topic of your research. Irrespective of what you actual research is on, I expect you to know the following by the end of the first year of Ph.D.: Basic engineering mathematics: You are supposed to know most of this from your undergraduate and/or masters studies. In any case, a good set of notes that will help you refresh important ideas from basic engineering mathematics can be found here . Classical mechanics: A thorough knowledge of classical mechanics is an essential prerequisite for understanding solid mechanics. This is a non-standard view; be that as it may, I expect you to be proficient in the basic concepts and techniques of classical mechanics. Besides being a very beautiful branch of mathematical physics, classical mechanics is so rich that it was, and continues to be, a wellspring of several key ideas in mathematics, physics and engineering. I highly recommend reading this book to learn classical mechanics. These will be guided self-study projects; you are expected to update me weekly on your progress. You are, of course, welcome to discuss these topics with me anytime. In the second year of your Ph.D., after you have completed the necessary coursework and your first APS (Annual Progress Seminar, when you will formally commit to a Ph.D. research topic and formulate a rough plan for your thesis), you are expected to do the following guided self-study, alongside your research: Solid mechanics: Needless to say, you are expected to know the basics of the mechanics of solids. You might have studied various aspects of this during your undergraduate and/or masters courses, but it is important that you get a birds-eye perspective of various aspects of solid mechanics by the end of your second year. A very nice and free online resource can be found here . Depending on your actual area of research, I may recommend additional reading material that will be useful for your research. But the three topics mentioned above are mandatory for all students who are interested in working with me. It is not as difficult as it appears, but even if it is, you will eventually find out that it benefits you immensely as you move forward with your Ph.D. If you're still game, you are welcome to join my group :) Current group members Here is a list of students who are working with me on various projects. More details coming soon! PhD candidates Divyesh Mistry (Dislocation Dynamics) Undergraduate students Prasanth YSS (Geometric Mechanics and Variational Integrators) Anurag Patel (Molecular Dynamics with PySPH; joint student with Prof. Prabhu Ramachandran) Aaron John Sabu (Mechanical Logic Gates) Short-term student rojects Supervised Learning Project (SLP) Karthik Anantharaman (Topology Optimization) Prasanth YSS (Optimal Control) Apoorva Singh Gangwar (Random Walks) Rushi Lotti (Neural Networks) M.Tech. Seminars Vinay Padmajan (Metamaterials) Former members Ninad Bhat ( Now: Masters student at Australian National University) Dharamveer Kumar ( Now: Ph.D. candidate at University of Minnesota) Sanjoy Kumar Mazumder ( Now: Ph.D. candidate at Purdue University)

The research in my group is broadly about understanding the physics of material behavior across various scales. Depending on the nature of the project, it can either be mathematics-intensive, or computation-intensive, or both. But before you commence your research in my group formally you need to be aware of the following guidelines: Scientific spirit: I expect you to fully assimilate the scientific spirit, understand the way scientific research is conducted and strive to embody the scientific spirit in your research. If you don't care strongly about the scientific spirit, then I don't think you should be doing a Ph.D. in the first place. You can find a very nice and simple introduction to the nature of the science in this link . Research ethics and integrity: It is imperative that you hold yourself to the highest ethical standards when it comes to research. Please read the guidelines given in this link to understand certain basic aspects of research ethics. Scientific integrity is an indispensable to doing good research; please be advised that there will be severe consequences in line with the institute policies if I find any evidence that you engaged in any form of academic misconduct. Growth mindset: Pursuing a Ph.D. is a serious time commitment from both your and my end. It is very probable that you may feel overwhelmed, overworked, or lost during the process. That is natural, and it is totally okay to feel like that. What is really important is that you learn how to cope with failures of various kinds, which are inevitable in your scientific journey. Rather than being dejected by failure, it really helps to embrace them as opportunities to re-evaluate the situation and improve yourself. If you join my group, you are always welcome to talk to me about any difficulty you may be facing. It is important for me that you feel welcome and find the atmosphere in the research group nurturing so that you may pursue your research to the best of your abilities. But I can't help you if you don't talk to me. While on this topic, I highly recommend reading Mindset: the new psychology of success by Carol Dweck. Work hard: Doing a Ph.D. in my group will definitely involve a lot of hard work. I can assure you that it will be quite challenging, but it will be well worth the effort in the end. My goal is to help you become a good and independent researcher by the time you graduate. If you don't like to work hard, I don't think you should join my group. I will, of course, make sure that you have the necessary resources and help to ensure that you enjoy the process, but there are no shortcuts in research: you need to work hard and you need to learn how to persist in the face of failures. In addition to the general guidelines mentioned above, you are also expected to take care of the following prerequisites on your own. You can, of course, talk to me if you need help with any of these. You are expected to learn the following by the end of the first semester of your Ph.D.: Scientific writing: The ability to write well and to express your ideas clearly is an integral part of doing good science. These skills do not sprout magically. They are rather acquired through deliberate effort and practice. You may find this resource to be a useful starting point. I will give you specific writing and presentation tasks over the course of your Ph.D. to help you develop these skills and learn the art of effective communication. LaTeX: The de-facto standard in preparing technical documents in science and engineering is the LaTeX typesetting system. You will be expected to turn in numerous reports over the course of your Ph.D., and, of course, communicate your reserach to other researchers in the form of peer-reviewed publications. Learning LaTeX will turn out to be very useful in this process. I expect you to learn how to create documents using LaTeX; you may consult this tutorial as a simple starting point. You can also use LaTeX to create presentations; see this tutorial to get started with using the Beamer package. Programming: I expect you have a hands-on knowledge of programming in Python irrespective of whether your project is theoretical or computational. A wonderful resource for learning Python is the video lecture series by Prof. Prabhu Ramachandran , who is a faculty member in the Aerospace Engineering department here at IIT Bombay. You are expected to know the material covered in the videos in the Basic-Programming-using-Python folder in the link give earlier. If your Ph.D. research is computational in nature, I expect you to know the basics of programming in C++, in addition to Python. A good tutorial for learning C++ can be found here . Plotting and visualization: Creating good plots and presenting data using effective visualization tools are an integral part of scientific communication. For quick and dirty plotting, I recommend learning Gnuplot; a tutorial can be found here . For creating publication quality plots, I recommend using PyPlot; you can find a good tutorial here . Scientific illustrations: Drawing good schematics provides a very elegant means to communicate various ideas and concepts in your scientific research. It is therefore important to learn how to create good illustrations. I recommend learning Ipe , which is a free and easy-to-use software for creating scientific illustrations. If you are more adventurous, you may optionally try learning the Tikz package; a basic set of tutorials can be found here . You will be doing a variety of coursework during the first year of your Ph.D. I will recommend various courses based on the topic of your research. Irrespective of what you actual research is on, I expect you to know the following by the end of the first year of Ph.D.: Basic engineering mathematics: You are supposed to know most of this from your undergraduate and/or masters studies. In any case, a good set of notes that will help you refresh important ideas from basic engineering mathematics can be found here . Classical mechanics: A thorough knowledge of classical mechanics is an essential prerequisite for understanding solid mechanics. This is a non-standard view; be that as it may, I expect you to be proficient in the basic concepts and techniques of classical mechanics. Besides being a very beautiful branch of mathematical physics, classical mechanics is so rich that it was, and continues to be, a wellspring of several key ideas in mathematics, physics and engineering. I highly recommend reading this book to learn classical mechanics. These will be guided self-study projects; you are expected to update me weekly on your progress. You are, of course, welcome to discuss these topics with me anytime. In the second year of your Ph.D., after you have completed the necessary coursework and your first APS (Annual Progress Seminar, when you will formally commit to a Ph.D. research topic and formulate a rough plan for your thesis), you are expected to do the following guided self-study, alongside your research: Solid mechanics: Needless to say, you are expected to know the basics of the mechanics of solids. You might have studied various aspects of this during your undergraduate and/or masters courses, but it is important that you get a birds-eye perspective of various aspects of solid mechanics by the end of your second year. A very nice and free online resource can be found here . Depending on your actual area of research, I may recommend additional reading material that will be useful for your research. But the three topics mentioned above are mandatory for all students who are interested in working with me. It is not as difficult as it appears, but even if it is, you will eventually find out that it benefits you immensely as you move forward with your Ph.D. If you're still game, you are welcome to join my group :)

docs.text: Details of the courses I teach at IIT Bombay can be found here. Note that this page will be continuously updated to reflect the latest course content. The lecture notes for the courses are still evolving. As a working solution , I have hosted some of the course material in separate webpages. PDF versions of the final lecture notes will be uploaded here as and when the notes reach some degree of maturity. AE 102 - Data Analysis and Interpretation Spring 2020 This is a freshman undergraduate course that aims to provide a basic introduction to probability theory and its applications to statistical inference. The larger goal of this course is to provide an introduction to critical thinking based on valid evidence in the presence of uncertainty. The course will feature both probabilistic simulation and probabilistic inference as alternative but mutually complementary approaches to extract meaningful information from data. Depending on the availability of time, the course will also feature an elementary introduction to certain aspects of machine learning. This course will be jointly taught by myself and Prof. Prabhu Ramachandran . I will be covering the theoretical aspects of the course and Prof. Prabhu Ramachandran will be teaching you everything that you need to know regarding programming in Python, as it applies to data analysis. The TAs for this course are Dinesh Adepu, Avinash More, Lotti Rushi, Thiruvadi Raghupathy and Rahul Jangid. Course Material All the course material will be uploaded on the course moodle. Some of the slides that I prepared for the course can be found below: Introduction: slides AE 337/AE 731 - Multiscale Modeling of Materials Fall 2020 This will be a fully online course due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic. The course policy document can be found here . More information will be shared here as the semester progresses. Fall 2018 This is a new graduate level elective dealing with various aspects of modeling the mechanical behavior of crystalline materials across various length and time scales, from atomistic to the continuum. Topics covered include basic ideas in Quantum, Classical, Statistical and Continuum Mechanics, along with relevant numerical techniques. Assignments for this course will be uploaded on the IITB Moodle. AE 486/AE 639 - Continuum Mechanics Course material Introduction: slides . Mathematical prerequisites : This webpage contains lecture notes providing an elementary introduction to essential topics in tensor algebra and analysis to get started with continuum mechanics. Note that this webpage is still under construction: it will be revised and updated over the course of this semester. Continuum Mechanics : This webpage is meant to provide a basic introduction to certain key ideas in continuum mechanics. Please note that this webpage is still under construction: it will be revised and updated over the course of this semester. Fall 2019 This time around, AE 639 will be taught using the flipped classroom model. All lectures will be video lectures, links for which will be uploaded on the IITB moodle. The actual lectures in the classroom will be discussion based. The primary reference for this course are the video lectures. The TAs for this course will be Lotti Rushi and Divyesh Mistry. Note that the classroom for this course is the Aerospace Annex Lecture Hall. Spring 2019 The TAs for this course this year are Sneha Cheryala and Lalit Bhola. The classes will be held in the Aerospace Annex Lecture Hall. Spring 2018 This is a senior undergraduate/beginning graduate elective in Continuum Mechanics. The course TA for this year is Ashish Kumar Bodla. Please refer to the most recent offering of the course for slides and/or lecture notes.
